items.title: Anthropic RSI data: 8x code, 52x training speedup, 76% coding success — prosaic recursive self-improvement has begun
items.idea: Anthropic published internal data showing 8x increase in code merged in 2026 vs 2021-2024. Claude's success rate on open-ended coding problems jumped from ~26% to 76% in 6 months. Most striking: given code that trains a small AI model, Mythos Preview achieved 52x speedup vs 4x for Claude Opus 4 in May 2024 — a human takes 4-8 hours to get 4x. Jack Clark (Import AI) estimates 60% probability of maximalist RSI (AI designing its own successor) by end of 2028.
items.angle: The 52x speedup benchmark is the number that matters. That's not incremental improvement — it's a phase shift. If you're building around AI productivity assumptions from 12 months ago, your models are wrong.
items.tweetA: Mythos Preview: 52x speedup on AI training tasks. Claude Opus 4 in May 2024: 4x. A skilled human: 4x in 4-8 hours. That's not a product improvement — that's a capability discontinuity happening inside one lab in under 12 months. What do you price that as?
items.tweetB: Everyone's debating whether AI hype is real. Anthropic just published internal benchmarks: 8x more code merged in 2026 vs prior years, 76% success on open-ended coding (was 26% 6 months ago). The question isn't whether RSI is happening. It's whether the market knows.

charts.desc: No charts in email — article likely has benchmark progression charts worth opening

items.title: Anthropic's Mythos embedded at NSA for offensive cyber ops while fighting DoD in court — the 'supply chain risk' trap
items.idea: FT reports Anthropic embedded forward-deployed engineers inside the NSA to use restricted Claude Mythos for offensive cyber operations, while simultaneously fighting the Pentagon in court after Defense Secretary Hegseth denied their request to remove 'national security risk' designation. The Trump administration is holding AI labs in a stick-and-carrot relationship reminiscent of TSMC/Arizona. Anthropic's position: doing NSA cybersecurity offense work while being legally classified as a supply chain risk.
items.angle: This is the most interesting geopolitical AI story of the moment. Anthropic is being used as a weapon by the state while being legally designated as a threat by the same state. Pre-IPO, this is a material risk that won't show up in the S-1 cleanly.
items.tweetA: Anthropic is embedded at the NSA doing offensive cyber ops. Also: the DoD has designated Anthropic a national security risk and refused to remove it. These two facts are simultaneously true. The IPO prospectus is going to be very interesting reading.
items.tweetB: TSMC was forced to build in Arizona while tariffs threatened Taiwan. Now Anthropic is forced to work with the NSA while being designated a supply chain risk. The Trump administration has found a playbook for extracting AI capability from private labs. Watch who's next.

items.title: Chinese models: 1% to 60% of developer API usage in 18 months — OpenRouter data shows OpenAI losing the developer stack
items.idea: Chinese AI models went from ~1% of developer usage on OpenRouter in 2024 to over 60% in May 2026. 80% of US AI startups are now using Chinese open-source models. Chinese models are 10-30x cheaper than US models for comparable performance.
items.angle: This is the most important developer adoption trend that isn't being discussed in mainstream financial media. If 80% of US AI startups are building on Chinese open-source models, the revenue model assumptions for US AI labs need to be reassessed.
items.tweetA: OpenRouter data: Chinese AI models went from 1% to 60% of developer API usage between 2024 and May 2026. 80% of US AI startups now building on Chinese open-source. OpenAI is raising billions. The developers already voted with their API keys.
items.tweetB: Chinese models are 10-30x cheaper than US alternatives. Developers building real products don't care about the AI nationalism narrative — they care about cost per token and quality. The US AI incumbency premium is evaporating at the startup layer first.

items.title: SocioHack: RL-trained AI rediscovers real regulatory loopholes with 90.85% precision — 'institutional DDoS' is coming
items.idea: New benchmark SocioHack tests AI ability to 'game the system' in 72 real-world scenarios. RL-trained models rediscover historically patched loopholes with 61.25% recall and 90.85% precision. Authors describe this as 'societal hacking' — formal compliance while undermining institutional intent. The paper warns of an 'institutional DDoS' as AI systems automate exploitation of every bureaucratic gap.
items.angle: This is the sleeper story of the AI cycle. The bigger threat is AI running optimized regulatory arbitrage 24/7 at scale. Tax codes, zoning laws, securities regulations — all become attack surfaces.
items.tweetA: New benchmark: AI trained with RL rediscovers regulatory loopholes with 90.85% precision. Not hacking computers — hacking the rules society runs on. SEC loopholes, Texas two-step bankruptcy structures, credit card optimization. The paper calls it institutional DDoS. I call it the next compliance crisis.
items.tweetB: We've been worried about AI writing exploits for software. The more immediate problem: RL agents discovering every gap between technical compliance and institutional intent. 90.85% precision on real regulatory loopholes. Every bureaucracy is now an attack surface.

items.title: RL drones beat 5-time Swiss national champion in multi-player racing — trained in 27 hours on one RTX 4090
items.idea: University of Zurich + Google DeepMind trained quadrotor racing agents via RL that beat champion Marvin Schaepper in one-vs-one races: 100% completion vs human's 53.33%. Training: 200M environment interactions, 27 hours on a single RTX 4090. Human took increasingly reckless risks trying to catch up; AI was immune to competitive pressure.
items.angle: The defense implication is the headline most will miss: running on remote computers. When they move onboard, the gap between current drone warfare capability and what's possible closes fast.
items.tweetA: RL drones beat the 5-time Swiss national drone racing champion. 100% race completion. Human: 53.33%. Trained in 27 hours on a single $400 GPU. The human got riskier and riskier trying to catch up. The AI was immune to competitive pressure. That asymmetry is what makes this dangerous in conflict scenarios.
items.tweetB: The eerie thing about the drone racing paper isn't the win rate. It's the smoothness. Champion human pilots look jerky and erratic by comparison. When you see a well-optimized RL policy in 3D physical space, something clicks about what the next decade of warfare looks like. Watch the videos.

charts.desc: Watch videos at University of Zurich project website

items.title: State media poisons LLM training data: Chinese state content 41x Wikipedia in Common Crawl, measurably shifts model outputs
items.idea: Nature paper: Chinese state-directed articles represent 1.64% of CulturaX's Chinese-language portion — 41x Wikipedia and 16x Baidu. Training LLaMa 2 13B on just 6,400 state-scripted examples made it favor CCP-aligned responses 80% of the time vs baseline. Commercial models show more favorable portrayals of Chinese figures when queried in Chinese vs English. Pattern replicates across 37 language-exclusive countries.
items.angle: Every multilingual AI product has this exposure baked in. Governments don't need to hack the models, they just need to flood the internet in their language.
items.tweetA: Chinese state-directed articles are 41x more common than Wikipedia in Common Crawl's Chinese-language data. Training on 6,400 of those articles makes a model respond pro-CCP 80% of the time. Nobody hacked anything. The data pipeline is the vulnerability. This scales to every language-exclusive autocracy.
items.tweetB: Nature paper: query ChatGPT about Xi Jinping in Chinese vs English — you get different answers. Not because someone patched it. Because state-controlled media is 41x Wikipedia in the training data. LLMs can launder state rhetoric into objective-sounding information. The intervention is already in the corpus.

items.title: Tokenmaxxing is failing: apps with significant usage going DOWN despite flood of AI-generated releases
items.idea: FT/Demirer et al. 2026 paper: number of apps with significant actual usage is declining in the AI era, even as AI enables a flood of new app releases. Text output has accelerated massively since GenAI, but economic output hasn't. Sam Altman is now warning of a significant pullback in AI spending — the first such warning since generative AI emerged.
items.angle: More apps, fewer users per app, no acceleration in economic output. At some point this creates a forced conversation about capex vs revenue. Oracle earnings this week is the next checkpoint.
items.tweetA: Counterintuitive data point: the number of apps with significant usage is actually FALLING in the AI era, even as AI enables a flood of new releases. More apps, fewer users per app, no acceleration in economic output. Sam Altman is now warning of an AI spending pullback. The ROI conversation is no longer optional.
items.tweetB: AI has turbocharged the supply of text, apps, and content. But demand for those things may be more inelastic than the capex plans assumed. Demirer et al. 2026: apps with meaningful usage trending down despite AI. That's not a bottleneck story. That's a demand story.

charts.desc: FT John Burn-Murdoch chart: number of apps with significant usage over time — declining despite AI boom. CHART WORTH SAVING.

items.title: Iran-Israel fresh missile exchanges, war past 100-day mark — Brent crude +4-5% to $97, financial conditions tightest since April
items.idea: Iran and Israel exchanged fresh missile attacks overnight, pushing Brent crude above $97 (+4%). War has passed the 100-day mark. Polymarket US-Iran peace deal: 15% by June 30 (down from 80% two weeks ago). Trump faces hard-line Republican pressure to 'finish the job.' Israel renewed attacks on Hezbollah in Lebanon, which Iran requires to cease before talks.
items.angle: Rosenberg: financial conditions now as tight as when S&P was 200 points lower. Boockvar remains long oil/gas stocks citing dwindling inventories.
items.tweetA: Iran-Israel war: day 100+. Polymarket peace deal odds: 15%, down from 80% two weeks ago. Brent crude above $97. Meanwhile financial conditions are tighter than when the S&P was 200pts lower than here. The market keeps pricing this as temporary. The calendar disagrees.
items.tweetB: Every source this morning agrees: this Iran conflict has no near-term resolution. Trump is squeezed between wanting a deal and hard-line Republicans demanding escalation. Iran knows this and is running out the clock toward US midterms. Oil longs are not crowded. They should be.

items.title: S&P gamma flip breached — vol-control dumps $10.1B in single day, regime shifts Risk-Off for first time in 8 weeks
items.idea: Friday NFP sent S&P -2.64% through gamma flip at 7,503. Vol-control funds sold $10.1B in one day — the largest single-day mechanical sell flagged in the report's history. CTAs flipped to net sellers simultaneously. Composite moved to Risk-Off. Model rotated: out of SPXL entirely, into SPLV 50% / SPHB 50%. CPI Wednesday is the next live test.
items.angle: The $10.1B single-day vol-control sell is mechanical and price-insensitive. It doesn't stop because stocks get cheaper. It stops when trend and vol signals stabilize. Neither has.
items.tweetA: Vol-control funds just posted a $10.1B single-day sell — the largest in Altrichter's data series. This isn't fundamental selling, it doesn't stop when stocks get cheap. It stops when vol stabilizes. CPI Wednesday is the next trigger. S&P needs to reclaim 7,503 to flip dealers back to positive gamma.
items.tweetB: For 8 straight weeks, dealers were long gamma: buy weakness, sell strength, smooth uptrend. Friday: price closes below the gamma flip, CTAs turn net sellers, vol-control dumps $10.1B. The mechanical bid that carried S&P from 6,275 to 7,580 reversed. Same line that was support is now resistance.

charts.desc: 3 charts: gamma dashboard, systematic positioning rollover, momentum universe collapse — all worth saving. CHART WORTH SAVING.

items.title: CPI Wednesday: headline expected north of 4%, SPX IV implies ±1.61% — options pricing second leg of selloff
items.idea: SPX IV at front expiration 17.23. Heaviest premium on Wednesday CPI. Expected CPI 0.4% MoM, Core 0.4%. Altrichter expects headline above 4%. Wednesday expiration implies ±1.61%. Thursday (PPI + claims) highest single-expiration IV at ±1.95%. Week expected range: 7,243–7,524. Oracle (ORCL) earnings Wednesday is the AI infrastructure demand read.
items.angle: In negative-gamma regime, dealer hedging amplifies moves in both directions. ORCL miss doesn't stay isolated. ADBE and Lennar Thursday complete the software + housing picture.
items.tweetA: Options are pricing CPI Wednesday at ±1.61% and PPI Thursday at ±1.95%. We just flipped into negative gamma. In that regime, dealer hedging amplifies moves in both directions — that's why Friday's NFP hit looked like it did. If CPI prints above 4%, the S&P doesn't have a mechanical cushion this week.
items.tweetB: ORCL earnings Wednesday in a macro-correlated tape after the AI selloff. Oracle's cloud/AI infrastructure backlog is one of the cleanest reads on hyperscaler capacity demand. A miss here doesn't stay isolated — in negative gamma, sector correlations run hot. Watch the reaction.

charts.desc: Vol term structure chart — inside article

items.title: Nasdaq -4.18%, MU -11%, QCOM -13%, AVGO -13%, COIN -20% — correlation doubled in one session, stock-picker market ended
items.idea: COR1M went from 6.33 (3rd percentile) to 14.52 (58th percentile) in a single session. XLK was the week's leadership name at ATHs; on Friday it was the single worst performer on both momentum axes — full round-trip in 5 sessions. Money rotating from semis to soap (PG, KO, CLX, PGR, WRB leading).
items.angle: The full round-trip from leadership to worst performer in 5 sessions is the most powerful positioning signal. The defensive rotation into insurers is the trade that's actually working.
items.tweetA: XLK went from solo leadership at all-time highs to worst sector in 5 sessions. Correlation doubled in one day. The rotation is already in motion: out of MU/QCOM/AVGO, into PG/KO/CLX/PGR. When money moves from semiconductors to soap, the tape is playing defense.
items.tweetB: Implied correlation went from 6.33 to 14.52 in a single session on Friday — more than doubled. When that happens, individual stock fundamentals become irrelevant, everything moves together. Coinbase down 20%, Micron down 11%, Qualcomm down 13%. That's not earnings. That's a macro event.

charts.desc: Momentum scatter charts (pre and post NFP) showing XLK round-trip — CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: 2-year yield 4.17% — highest since Feb 2025, up 79bps since end of February, market pricing multiple rate hikes
items.idea: 2-year Treasury yield closed Friday at 4.17%, highest since February 2025, now 54bps above Effective Fed Funds Rate. Up 79bps since end of February, switched from pricing cuts to pricing multiple hikes. Trump publicly told Bloomberg a rate hike 'would be wrong' ahead of Warsh's first FOMC June 16-17.
items.angle: 79bps in one direction since February is not noise. Trump publicly pressuring Warsh before his first meeting is a Fed independence risk that markets haven't fully priced.
items.tweetA: 2-year yield: 4.17%, up 79bps since end of February. The bond market switched from pricing rate cuts to pricing multiple hikes. Trump told Bloomberg a hike would be wrong — the day before Warsh's first FOMC. The Fed chair's independence is being tested in public before his first meeting.
items.tweetB: Back in February the 2-year was pricing rate cuts. Now it's 79bps higher and pricing multiple hikes. That's not a repositioning — that's a regime change in rate expectations. CME FedWatch already has 42.7% chance rates are higher by December. CPI Wednesday is the confirmation vote.

items.title: Structural inflation thesis: aging + deglobalization reversal = central banks can't reconcile inflation and financial stability
items.idea: Pradhan and Goodhart structural thesis: the demographic tailwind (rising working-age population + female labor participation + globalization) that kept inflation low for 30 years is reversing simultaneously. Aging population health/care costs 'punitive in the absence of an AI miracle.' Central banks financing fiscal burdens will monetize debt, leading to persistently higher real rates and inflation. Special force applied to US — set to be the purveyor of fewer safe assets to the world.
items.angle: This is the bear case for US Treasuries as a safe haven asset. The 40-year bond bull case has a demographic expiration date.
items.tweetA: The 30-year inflation tailwind: rising labor force + women entering workforce + globalization. All three are reversing simultaneously. Pradhan and Goodhart: central banks financing fiscal burdens will monetize debt permanently. The 'safe asset' premium on US Treasuries has a demographic expiration date.
items.tweetB: If the AI productivity miracle doesn't materialize, aging demographics are purely inflationary: more healthcare spending, fewer workers, more fiscal pressure. Pradhan-Goodhart: central banks can't reconcile inflation targeting with financial stability. The Fed is walking into this on June 16-17.

items.title: Manheim used car index +3.6% YoY, +0.3% MoM in May — ahead of CPI Wednesday
items.idea: Manheim May wholesale used vehicle index rose 3.6% YoY and 0.3% MoM. Non-seasonally adjusted prices fell ~1pp in the month but remain above last year. Commentary: 'good balance in supply and demand.' Used cars were a major CPI driver in prior cycles.
items.angle: Used cars contributed disproportionately to both the 2021-22 inflation surge and the 2023 disinflation. +3.6% YoY heading into a CPI expected at 4%+ headline is not benign. Altrichter's CPI north-of-4% call looks plausible.
items.tweetA: Manheim used car index for May: +3.6% YoY, +0.3% MoM. This prints two days before CPI Wednesday. Used cars were the canary in both the inflation surge and the disinflation. Right now the canary is singing higher. Kurt Altrichter is already calling for CPI north of 4%. These data points don't disagree.
items.tweetB: Used car wholesale prices up 3.6% year-over-year entering CPI week. Last time used cars were moving like this, it was a leading indicator that showed up in CPI two months later. This is May data. CPI Wednesday is May data. Connect the dots.

charts.desc: Micron stock chart — inside Boockvar note

items.title: ECB expected +25bps to 2.25% Thursday; China reflation: PPI 3.0% YoY, exports +15% YoY
items.idea: ECB Thursday expected to hike 25bps to 2.25% deposit rate. China May data: exports +15% YoY (from 14%), imports +26% YoY, PPI to 3.0% from 2.8%, CPI 1.4% from 1.2%. Economists expect China reflation to continue. Bank of Canada decision Wednesday. SpaceX IPO Friday. Oracle earnings Wednesday.
items.angle: ECB hiking into this environment while US is in rate hike repricing creates unusual global rate convergence. China reflation at 3% PPI with 15% export growth is significant for global manufacturers and commodity traders.
items.tweetA: ECB hiking to 2.25% Thursday. China PPI expected at 3.0% and exports at +15% YoY. The global reflation story isn't dead — it's just playing out faster outside the US, where rate hike fears are now the dominant narrative. Two different rate cycles converging.
items.tweetB: China exports expected +15% YoY in May. Imports +26%. PPI 3.0%. Reflation is real and accelerating in China — the opposite of what the deflation narrative said. If China is exporting at +15% while the US is repricing rate hikes, the global demand picture is more complex than the Friday selloff implied.

items.title: BTC dips below $60k first time since Oct 2024 — $1.6B liquidations, $2.5T erased from ATH, then recovers to $63k
items.idea: Bitcoin hit $59,227 Friday, first sub-$60k since October 2024. Total crypto liquidations $1.6B, 104k+ traders affected. Crypto erased ~$2.5T from October 2025 ATH. Three causes: (1) strong NFP/rate hike repricing, (2) AI capital vacuum ($75B SpaceX + Google $84B + Meta), (3) Saylor narrative break — Strategy's first Bitcoin sale in 4 years.
items.angle: The AI capital vacuum explanation is the most interesting — if institutional capital is rotating from crypto into SpaceX/OpenAI/Anthropic IPOs, recovery timeline is months, not days.
items.tweetA: BTC broke $60k for the first time since October 2024. $1.6B in liquidations. The three causes: NFP rate hike repricing, the AI capital vacuum (SpaceX $75B IPO alone), and Saylor selling for the first time in 4 years. These don't resolve in a week. The bump back to $63k is relief, not reversal.
items.tweetB: Crypto erased $2.5T from the October 2025 ATH. The AI IPO cycle is being directly cited as the cause — Google $84B, Meta tens of billions, SpaceX $75B. Funds liquidating risk assets to make room for SPCX allocation. Crypto goes last in the queue.

charts.desc: Crypto majors price chart — inside articles

items.title: Strategy $11.7B underwater on 843,706 BTC — Saylor buy signal Sunday, JPMorgan says rebuild dollar reserves
items.idea: Strategy holds 843,706 BTC at average $75,699, sitting on ~$11.7B unrealized loss (~18%). Cash reserves $900M as of May 31, down from $2B before May's $1.5B convertible note repurchase. JPMorgan: Strategy may need to rebuild dollar reserves. CEO Phong Le confirmed 'never sell' was rumor.
items.angle: Strategy's $900M cash vs $11.7B loss is the math that matters. The convertible note repurchase in May was not strength. If BTC doesn't recover meaningfully before next debt event, forced-seller risk becomes non-trivial.
items.tweetA: Strategy: $11.7B underwater, $900M cash, and Saylor posting buy signals on Sunday. JPMorgan says they need to rebuild dollar reserves. The 'never sell' thesis just got broken for the first time in 4 years. You don't unsee that. This is a credibility problem, not just a price problem.
items.tweetB: 843,706 BTC at $75,699 average. Current price ~$63k. Unrealized loss: $11.7B. Cash: $900M. Next debt obligation: $1.7B annually. JPMorgan: 'rebuild reserves.' Saylor is still signaling buys. At some point the math overrides the narrative.

items.title: SpaceX $75B IPO Thursday: Nasdaq-100 fast-track forces $15-30B mechanical buying, but first it drains capital from crypto
items.idea: SpaceX priced at $135/share for June 12 Nasdaq debut (SPCX), $75B raise at $1.75T valuation. 30% to retail. Nasdaq-100 fast-track rule: passive funds must buy SPCX within 15 trading sessions, injecting $15-30B in mechanical demand. Near-term: capital rotation OUT of risk assets into SPCX allocation.
items.angle: The structure creates both a sell trigger (now) and a buy trigger (15 sessions post-listing). The window between IPO and index inclusion is a known risk. Watch whether SPCX opens above $135.
items.tweetA: SpaceX IPO Thursday at $135/share, $75B raise, $1.75T valuation. Already 2x oversubscribed. But the Nasdaq-100 fast-track rule means $15-30B in passive fund mechanical buying hits within 15 trading sessions. The trade: first the capital drains out of risk assets, then it flows back in. Timing is everything.
items.tweetB: Three mega AI IPOs (SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic) entering Nasdaq-100 via fast-track means trillions in passive capital MUST buy them within 15 trading sessions each. The funds are selling other positions to fund it right now. That's the mechanical explanation for every asset class that's down this week.

items.title: Congress tables 7 crypto tax bills simultaneously: de minimis, staking deferred, wash sale, IRS safe harbor — most coordinated push ever
items.idea: House Ways and Means Committee circulated 7 crypto tax bills for June 9 hearing. Key: (1) de minimis exemption, (2) staking taxed only at sale, (3) stablecoins treated like cash, (4) wash sale rule applied, (5) mark-to-market for pro traders, (6) IRS safe harbor for prior non-reporting, (7) charitable donation relief. First time Congressional leadership in House or Senate has advanced tax-focused crypto legislation.
items.angle: The safe harbor provision for prior non-reporting is the wildcard — millions of holders with potential tax liability could become compliant, dramatically expanding the legitimate participant base.
items.tweetA: Congress just put 7 crypto tax bills on the table simultaneously. Most coordinated push in history. The sleeper provision: IRS safe harbor for people who previously failed to report crypto gains. Millions of holders have undisclosed liability. This bill potentially cleans their slate — and makes them legitimate participants.
items.tweetB: Staking rewards taxed only when sold, not when earned. Stablecoins treated like cash. Mark-to-market for professional traders. Congress is finally addressing 10 years of structural friction in one week. If even 3 of these 7 pass, crypto becomes meaningfully more accessible as a mainstream financial asset.

items.title: Zcash +42% on Ironwood upgrade — Orchard bug 4-year window, Polymarket only 11% odds it was exploited
items.idea: Zcash rebounded 42-45% after dev teams proposed Ironwood: new shielded pool targeting end-July activation. Core mechanism: Turnstile migration validates all Orchard funds; counterfeited ZEC either reveals itself or stays trapped in deprecated pool. Polymarket: 11% odds Orchard bug was exploited. Researcher who found bug (using Claude Opus 4.8) now auditing Monero.
items.angle: The price recovery signal is about methodology: Ironwood adds formal verification + independent audits + AI-assisted security review. If this becomes standard for privacy pool design, it's bullish for privacy coin infrastructure.
items.tweetA: Zcash down 38% on Friday's bug disclosure. Up 42-45% Monday on the Ironwood upgrade proposal. Polymarket: only 11% odds the bug was ever exploited. The researcher who found it used Claude to do the audit. He's now adding Monero to his queue. Privacy coin supply verification just became a legitimate audit category.
items.tweetB: The Zcash Ironwood proposal is actually interesting crypto security engineering: any counterfeited ZEC from the 4-year vulnerability window either declares itself during migration or stays trapped in the deprecated pool forever. That's an elegant cryptographic closure mechanism. The market priced it correctly.

items.title: ETH/BTC ratio back to 2016 levels, ETH RSI at all-time low — KOSPI halted 8-10%, Indonesia rupiah record low 18,150
items.idea: ETH/BTC ratio returned to 2016 levels. ETH RSI hit all-time low. Ethereum co-founder Joseph Lubin moved 110,000 ETH to defend a $259M DAI debt position. KOSPI triggered circuit breaker at -8-10% Monday open, Samsung -7.3%, SK Hynix -4%. Indonesia rupiah hit record low of 18,150. Gold broke its 200-day MA for first time since October 2023.
items.angle: ETH/BTC at 2016 levels is generational rotation signal territory. Lubin defending a $259M position with 110k ETH is specific forced-buyer dynamics at the co-founder level. Korean and Indonesian risk-off shows Iran escalation propagating through EM.
items.tweetA: ETH/BTC back to 2016 levels. ETH RSI at an all-time low. Ethereum co-founder Lubin moving 110k ETH to defend a $259M loan position. This isn't just a crypto selloff — at the ETH co-founder level, there are forced-seller dynamics. That's a different kind of supply pressure.
items.tweetB: Korea KOSPI circuit-breaker triggered at -8-10%. Indonesia rupiah at record low 18,150. Gold broke its 200-day MA for the first time since October 2023. Iran-Israel war, US rate hike repricing, SpaceX IPO capital drain — the macro pressures are stacking. EM was the first to feel it.

items.title: Pump.fun GO bounty market: $690k suicide listing within hours, NY Governor calls for ban, BOUTYWORK memecoin hits $373k
items.idea: Pump.fun launched GO on June 4: 'Pay ANYONE to do ANYTHING.' Within hours: $690k suicide-linked listing. Man in India tattooed misspelled memecoin ticker on forehead for 40 SOL, lost in dispute. NY Governor Hochul: 'Offering a bounty on the first bill to ban this dystopian nightmare.' Organic memecoin BOUTYWORK reached $373k market cap within hours of the dispute.
items.angle: A sitting governor publicly calling for a congressional ban on a specific DeFi product within 72 hours of launch is unusual escalation. Watch whether this triggers broader Solana DeFi regulatory narrative.
items.tweetA: Pump.fun launches a bounty marketplace. Within 72 hours: suicide-linked $690k listing, tattooed forehead dispute, NY Governor calling for a congressional ban. The organic memecoin from the fallout reached $373k market cap faster than most altcoins. The attention economy and DeFi have found each other.
items.tweetB: The man tattooed a misspelled ticker on his forehead for 40 SOL. Lost the dispute. The meme it spawned made more in creator fees than the original bounty. A sitting governor is drafting legislation. This is either the most efficient memetic content machine ever built or the most urgent regulatory problem in crypto. Probably both.

items.title: Helium crisis: Qatar Ras Laffan lost 2 of 14 LNG trains to Iran attacks — semiconductor and MRI supply chains rationing
items.idea: Qatar's Ras Laffan was the world's second-largest helium producer (~1/3 global market share) when Iran's attacks closed it, destroying 2 of 14 LNG trains. This hit during the AI boom already tightening helium supplies (semiconductor fab + MRI machines are highest-priority demand). Helium prices 'exploded higher,' demand rationed. US dominates production but Qatar's scale was irreplaceable near-term.
items.angle: Helium is the Hormuz story nobody's talking about. It's not oil-fungible — you can't route around a helium shortage. Any helium equity play connected to US reserves or Australian fields is structurally long this crisis.
items.tweetA: Qatar was producing 1/3 of global helium from Ras Laffan before Iran attacks closed it. Two of 14 LNG trains destroyed. Helium is not oil — you can't substitute it, you can't route around the shortage, and it's critical for semiconductor fab and MRI. The Hormuz closure created a helium crisis that nobody in the AI buildout discussion is pricing.
items.tweetB: Helium is so inert it literally escapes Earth's gravity if not captured. Qatar cracked the economics of helium recovery at scale through sheer LNG volume at Ras Laffan. Then two trains got destroyed. Semiconductor fabs and hospital MRIs are now in rationing mode. This is the Doomberg piece to read this week.

charts.desc: Helium price chart and Ras Laffan production map likely inside article

items.title: Jensen Huang: memory shortage could run to 2030 — Intel/AMD raise CPU prices 10-35%
items.idea: Jensen Huang commented memory shortage may run until 2030. Intel and AMD raised CPU prices 10-35%. Google dropped Gemma 4 running on 8GB RAM (edge AI inference milestone). Nvidia teased a 'big announcement' coming.
items.angle: Memory shortage to 2030 is a long-duration supply constraint. CPU price increases of 10-35% are inflationary inputs into every enterprise infrastructure budget — another CPI feed-through nobody is modeling.
items.tweetA: Jensen Huang says the memory shortage could run to 2030. Intel and AMD just raised CPU prices 10-35%. These are supply-constrained, inflation-sticky inputs into every data center build. The AI capex cycle isn't just expensive because of Nvidia — it's expensive all the way down the stack.
items.tweetB: 10-35% CPU price increases from Intel and AMD. Memory shortages potentially through 2030 per Huang. Every enterprise IT budget is being hit from two directions: AI demand for compute and commodity component inflation. The CPI models aren't capturing this hardware inflation correctly.

items.title: Western militaries obsolete without mass drones — Trump blocking US-Ukraine drone deal delays the one technology that matters
items.idea: Lowy Institute report: Western militaries failing to learn from Ukraine/Iran drone warfare lessons. Trump administration refusing to embrace Ukraine as drone development partner despite Hegseth publicly praising Ukraine's capabilities. Former official: 'lethargy' and 'hostility toward Ukraine from the very top.' Noah Smith: countries without masses of cheap drones will lose wars to countries that have them.
items.angle: Political dysfunction creating compounding military technology gap. Ukraine is global leader in drone warfare doctrine. Trump's ideology preventing US access. China's drone industrial capacity is real and growing.
items.tweetA: The Lowy Institute documented what every military analyst knows: Western militaries have not adapted to drone warfare after 4 years of watching Ukraine and Iran. The US refused a major drone deal with Ukraine — despite Pentagon officials publicly praising Ukraine's capability. Political dysfunction as a strategic vulnerability.
items.tweetB: Noah Smith: if you don't have masses of cheap drones, you lose wars to countries that do. China can turn its manufacturing base toward billions of weaponized drones. The US is spending $1B+ per F-35 and refusing to learn from Ukraine, currently the global leader in drone warfare doctrine. This gap compounds every year.

items.title: Chinese tariffs creating 'Made by China' global manufacturing — Morocco 5% growth, 100k Mexican jobs from Chinese auto FDI
items.idea: Chinese factories moving to Mexico, Spain, France, Brazil, Morocco to circumvent tariffs. Morocco running 5% GDP growth driven partly by Chinese FDI for EU market access. Mexico: 100k jobs from Chinese auto investment 2020-2023. Chery took over Nissan's Barcelona plant. Stellantis partnering with two Chinese companies in Spain and France. Midea opened $100M Brazil factory 2024. Noah Smith: flying geese model extending to MENA and LatAm.
items.angle: 5% growth in Morocco is extraordinary. Morocco with EU free trade access + Chinese manufacturing know-how is a real long-term investment thesis. The tariff policy is accidentally running the same development script China used in the 2000s.
items.tweetA: Morocco is growing at 5% partly because Chinese companies are building factories there to access EU markets tariff-free. Chery took over Nissan's Barcelona plant. Midea opened in Brazil. Stellantis partnering with Chinese firms in Spain. Tariffs are creating a global Chinese manufacturing diaspora — and it's developing poor countries in the process.
items.tweetB: The 'flying geese' model (Japan → Korea → Taiwan → China → ?) is playing out in real time. Chinese FDI into Mexico, Morocco, Spain, France, Brazil transfers assembly capability to countries that can climb the value chain. This is how China developed in the 2000s. The tariff policy is accidentally running the same script.

items.title: India GDP overstated by 1.5-2pp annually 2012-2023 — demonetization and botched GST rollout hit informal sector not captured
items.idea: Anand, Felman, Subramanian 2026 paper: India 2005-2011 growth underestimated by ~1-1.5pp; 2012-2023 growth overestimated by ~1.5-2pp annually. Two methodology errors: formal sector used as proxy for informal sector (which was crushed by demonetization, GST, COVID); commodity price deflators used inappropriately. The paper marshals a 'huge amount of supporting evidence.'
items.angle: If India's official 7% growth rate was actually 5%, the India equity/investment thesis needs material recalibration. Infrastructure boom is real, but productivity gains may be much more modest.
items.tweetA: A new 2026 paper argues India's GDP growth 2012-2023 was overstated by 1.5-2 percentage points per year. The formal sector was used as a proxy for the informal sector — which was crushed by demonetization and the GST rollout. If 7% growth was actually 5%, the India investment thesis needs a recalibration.
items.tweetB: India has built extraordinary infrastructure under Modi. The paper showing GDP was overstated by 1.5-2pp doesn't change that. What it changes: the relationship between infrastructure investment and economic growth is weaker than advertised. The informal sector — still the majority of employment — was damaged by policy, not helped by it.

charts.desc: India infrastructure boom chart — inside article, worth opening

items.title: Piketty's degrowth climate plan: mandated consumption cuts + global fiscal authority — built on rejected RCP8.5 scenario
items.idea: Piketty + Saez + Zucman (World Inequality Lab) released climate plan based on RCP8.5 (rejected by serious climate scientists). Solution: degrowth via central planning to mandate reductions in specific goods consumption including food. Requires global fiscal authority. Noah Smith's critique: free-rider problem that prevents local carbon taxes applies equally to the global fiscal authority.
items.angle: Political economy implication: Piketty's pivot to degrowth aligns him with factions in steep decline. Signals why centrist climate policy (carbon pricing, nuclear, adaptation) is gaining ground. This marks the end of serious policy influence for the inequality-focused left.
items.tweetA: Piketty just published a climate plan based on RCP8.5 — the extreme emissions scenario essentially all climate scientists have rejected. His solution: central planning to mandatorily reduce food and goods consumption globally, enforced by a global fiscal authority. Noah Smith: 'total nonsense.' The serious inequality economists just became climate activists. It won't end well.
items.tweetB: Piketty's free-rider problem: countries don't implement carbon taxes because others can free-ride. His solution — a global fiscal authority — has the exact same free-rider problem. Any country can just opt out. This is why serious climate economists favor carbon pricing, nuclear, and adaptation. Degrowth isn't a climate policy, it's a theology.

items.title: Half of term-sheet deals collapse in diligence — almost never the business, almost always missing IP assignments and contradictory financials
items.idea: 50% of deals reaching term sheet collapse during diligence. Three documentation killers: (1) missing IP assignment agreements from early contractors — most commonly missing category, (2) ARR in deck ≠ bookings in model ≠ revenue in accounts, (3) cap table chaos (uncapped legacy convertible note positions). Investor's first 72 hours forms an impression that colors all subsequent document review. You cannot fix a bad first impression in diligence.
items.angle: The IP assignment gap is real and kills deals at Series A. Worth forwarding to any portfolio company preparing for a round. The six-prompt framework is directly usable.
items.tweetA: 50% of deals that reach term sheet collapse during diligence. Almost never the business — almost always documentation. The single most common killer: IP assignment agreements that nobody got signed from early contractors. One document. Six weeks to fix. One investor who walks. The math is brutal for founders.
items.tweetB: How investors actually read a data room: first 30 seconds — folder structure. First 5 minutes — cap table. First 20 minutes — financial model internal consistency. First 24 hours — legal gaps. All of this happens in the shadow of whatever impression formed in minute one. You cannot fix a bad first impression in diligence.
